g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore the six categories of system calls that form the foundation of operating system services.
System calls are how programs request services from the operating system. They're the interface between user programs and the OS kernel.
g.text: The bridge between user programs and the OS kernel. Programs cannot directly access hardware
or manage resources—they must request OS services through system calls.

g.data-narration: System calls are grouped into six major categories: Process Control, File Management, Device Management, Information Maintenance, Communication, and Protection.
Process Control handles creating and managing processes. fork() duplicates the current process, while exec() loads a new program into memory.
Key process control calls include fork, exec, exit, wait, getpid, kill, and abort. Windows equivalents like CreateProcess serve the same purpose.
File Management handles creating, reading, writing, and deleting files. The flow is open → read/write → close, using file descriptors.
g.text: Other calls: create(), delete(), lseek(), stat(), chmod(), chown()

g.data-narration: Device Management handles I/O devices. In UNIX, devices are treated as files in /dev—terminals, hard drives, and even /dev/null all use the same read/write calls.
g.text: UNIX treats devices as special files in /dev—same system calls work for files and devices:
Uses: open, read, write, ioctl, mmap

g.data-narration: Information Maintenance transfers info between programs and OS. Key calls: getpid, getuid, time, sleep, and uname for system information.
Communication has two models: Message Passing sends data through the kernel via pipes, sockets, or message queues. The OS handles synchronization.
Shared Memory allows direct memory access between processes—faster but requires manual synchronization. Uses shmget, shmat, and shmdt calls.
Protection controls resource access with chmod for permissions, chown for ownership, umask for defaults, and chroot for sandboxing.
Here's a complete summary: all six categories with their primary UNIX and Windows system call equivalents for each type of service.
Key takeaways: System calls are your gateway to OS services. Master fork/exec for processes, open/read/write/close for files, and understand both IPC models.
g.text: 6 categories cover ALL OS services programs can request
fork() + exec() = standard way to create and run new programs
File operations follow: open → read/write → close pattern
Message passing: easier sync, slower | Shared memory: faster, manual sync
UNIX "everything is a file" = same calls for files and devices
Protection: chmod, chown control file access permissions

g.data-narration: For exams: memorize all 6 categories, know 3-4 calls per category, understand fork/exec workflow, and compare message passing vs shared memory!
Congratulations! You've completed the system calls overview. These 6 categories are your roadmap to understanding how programs interact with the operating system.
Now you understand how every program operation—saving files, launching apps, network communication—maps to one of these six categories.
g.text: Every program operation maps to one of these 6 categories • You've got this!
